step.title: Step 54
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 122, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 121 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 4.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the active agent (W center, green border) is now at the top-left corner of the left panel. The left c-frame is below it. The other agent (B center) is in the connector.
I've been moving the active agent around without making progress toward the goal. I need to rethink what the goal is.
Let me look at the current segmentation to understand the state better.
Current state from image:
- Active agent (W center) at top-left of left panel
- Left c-frame below it in left panel
- Other agent (B center) in connector
- Right c-frame in right panel
I've used 121 actions and haven't solved the level. I need to figure out the goal quickly.
Let me think about what configurations could be the goal:
1. Both agents inside their respective c-frames
2. Agents in specific positions relative to c-frames
3. Something about matching colors
Given that:
- Left c-frame has c border and w interior
- Right c-frame has c border and w interior
- Active agent has W center
- Other agent has B center
Maybe the goal is to have:
- W center agent inside a c-frame (matching W to w interior)
- B center agent inside a c-frame (but B doesn't match w)
Or maybe the goal is to have both agents in the same panel?
Or maybe the goal is to have the agents in specific positions, not inside c-frames.
Let me try a different approach. Let me click on the other agent (B center) to activate it, and see if I can move it into the right c-frame.
Actually, I realize I've been focusing on the wrong thing. Let me look at the initial state again. The left agent was inside the left c-frame with a c center. The right agent was outside the right c-frame with a W center.
Maybe the goal is to have:
- Left agent inside left c-frame with c center (already satisfied initially)
- Right agent inside right c-frame with W center (needs to be done)
But the right agent is in the left panel, and the right c-frame is in the right panel. How can the right agent get to the right c-frame?
Wait, maybe I need to click on the right agent to activate it, then move it through the connector to the right panel, and then into the right c-frame.
But earlier, when I moved an agent into the connector, the game ended. Unless the game ended for a different reason (like pushing the c-frame out of position).
Let me try clicking on the other agent (B center) to activate it, and then move it RIGHT into the right panel.
Actually, looking at the image, the other agent is already in the connector. If I activate it and move RIGHT, it should enter the right panel.
Let me try that.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== CURRENT STATE ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20:
        boundary = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
        cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], children={n['children']}")

# Click on the other agent (B center) in the connector to activate it
# It's at rows 30-32, cols 27-29
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 28}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
